summary:
The patient’s symptoms—neck stiffness and locking—are consistent with an acute dystonic crisis due to dopamine blockade from enhanced antipsychotic use. Benztropine should be administered promptly because it effectively counteracts cholinergic excess caused by dopaminergic inhibition during such episodes.

Okay, I need to summarize Agent1's reasoning based strictly on their provided content while adhering exactly to two-sentence constraints focused only on clinically significant elements related directly to this case.

First sentence must identify what condition fits best given presentation and context—the core diagnosis being "acute dystonic crisis" linked through causation via new/high-dose antipsychotics causing dopamine receptor blockage leading to muscular rigidity/stiffness affecting specific neck musculature (sternocleidomastoid/trapezius).

Second sentence needs clearly state why treatment choice makes sense—that benztropine works here because its central anticholinergics reverse the imbalance resulting from reduced dopaminergic activity seen in these crises.

I will avoid mentioning unrelated details like asthma/albuterol since they don't contribute meaningfully to current symptom pathophysiology; focus remains solely on neurological mechanism tied directly to medications used post-hallucination onset.
